Post_Text: A supply line let go on a Friday night. It ran sixty hours into an empty school. The doors were locked. The alarm was set. Nobody did anything wrong. The first floor was still underwater Tuesday. Here's the fifty-dollar fix: [LINK]
A supply line let go on a Friday night. It ran for sixty hours into an empty building. Nobody was there to hear it. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Same pipe. Same water pressure. Same defect. In an occupied building it's a mop and a phone call. In an empty one over a long weekend it's a gut renovation. The only thing that changed was how long it ran: [LINK]

Water is the most frequent and most preventable property loss at schools. Here is what to monitor, where to put it, and what your carrier may pay you to install it. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: School leaders plan for fire and storms. The loss that actually drains the budget is quieter, more common, and more preventable. Water never announces itself. Here's where it hides at a school: [LINK]

A sensor that pings an inbox nobody reads on a Sunday has prevented nothing. The device is half the system. The response plan is the other half. Most schools buy the puck and skip the part that matters: [LINK]
Your carrier does not want to pay your water claim either. Some will give you premium credits, free sensors, or send someone to walk your building. Most schools never find out. Here's why: [LINK]

The credit sits there unused. The loss-control visit never gets scheduled. Not because your broker is hiding it. Because finding it is work, and your account got priced as too small to bother. Then one Tuesday the first floor is underwater: [LINK]

Walk your building in your head. The water heater, the closet under the server, the restroom at the far end of an empty wing. A line lets go Friday night over a long weekend. How long would it run before anyone knew? And has your broker ever asked to walk the building? [LINK]
